results.possibleIdentity: Lucio Pozzi (born 1935, Milan, Italy) is a multidisciplinary artist who moved to the United States in 1962. He is a prominent figure in the New York avant-garde scene, known for his work in painting, performance, installation, video, and sculpture. He was active during the late 1960s and 1970s, producing notable works such as the 'Triangles—Red' series (1969) and 'The Level Group' (early 1970s). He is explicitly documented in the archives of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, specifically noted in records from 1966–1968 regarding technical matchings between artists and engineers. His career includes solo exhibitions at the Museum of Modern Art (1978), participation in Documenta 6 (1977), and the Venice Biennale (1980). Beyond his art practice, Pozzi founded the magazine 'New Observations' in the mid-1970s and has taught at institutions such as Cooper Union, Yale University, Princeton University, and the School of Visual Arts. He currently divides his time between Hudson, New York, and Valeggio sul Mincio, Italy.

results.possibleIdentity: Wade Zimmerman is a prominent New York-based architectural photographer and designer who has been active since at least the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is well-known for documenting the works of world-renowned architects such as Christian de Portzamparc, Rafael Moneo, David Adjaye, Richard Meier, and Emilio Ambasz. Early in his career, he worked as a designer, notably credited as the designer for the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition catalog 'Another Chance for Housing: Low-Rise Alternatives' (1973), a project involving the Institute for Architecture and Urban Studies (IAUS) and the New York State Urban Development Corporation. His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement is supported by his photographic documentation of key interdisciplinary projects, such as Fujiko Nakaya's fog sculpture at the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, which was a landmark E.A.T. collaboration. Zimmerman's photographic style is noted for being influenced by the works of American artists Edward Hopper and Charles Sheeler, and he has been cited in professional architectural journals as one of the world's leading architectural photographers.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Boardman Howard (1896–1983), often known as Robert Howard or Bob Howard, was a prominent American sculptor, painter, and muralist active primarily in the San Francisco Bay Area. He was known for his modernist sculptures, mobiles, and large-scale public art, including the bas-relief phoenix on Coit Tower. Howard had a career-long interest in the intersection of art, engineering, and machinery, often experimenting with industrial materials like fiberglass and plastics in his work during the 1960s and 1970s. He is listed among the artists who utilized Lippincott Inc. (a major fabricator for large-scale avant-garde sculpture) for his work between 1966 and 1970, a period closely associated with the activities of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was part of a distinguished artistic family, married to sculptor Adeline Kent and the brother of artists Charles and John Langley Howard.

Robert (Bob) Howard (1923–2015) was a prolific American engineer and inventor who was a close associate of Billy Klüver, the co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). While primarily known as a technology pioneer—specifically for inventing the dot-matrix printer and founding Centronics and Presstek—Howard was a significant figure in the circle of collaborators that bridged the gap between engineering and the arts. He co-authored the book 'Leadership the Hard Way' with Klüver, which reflects on their long-standing partnership. In the context of E.A.T., which promoted the fusion of art and technology, Howard represents the 'engineering' half of the artist-engineer collaboration that defined the organization's mission during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Robert Howard was a contributor and translator associated with 'Aspen 5+6' (1967), a multimedia magazine issue titled 'The Experiments in Art and Technology Issue,' edited by Brian O'Doherty. Howard provided the translation for a recorded excerpt of Alain Robbe-Grillet’s 'Jealousy' included in the issue. This specific publication served as a 'time capsule' for the E.A.T. movement, featuring contributions from artists and thinkers like Robert Rauschenberg, John Cage, and Sol LeWitt, placing this Robert Howard directly within the organization's collaborative network during its peak activity in the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Elaine Galen (born c. 1928) is an American painter, sculptor, and educator active from the late 1950s through the present. She is recognized for a diverse body of work that includes abstract expressionist landscapes, sculptures, and interdisciplinary collaborations. Galen gained early prominence when her painting "Naturescape" was featured in the 1961 Whitney Museum Biennial. Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, she held solo exhibitions at major institutions including the Brooklyn Museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Art Institute of Chicago. In the 1970s, she expanded into "Body Sculpture," creating wearable steel works such as the "Jawbone Helmet," which received first prize from the American Iron & Steel Institute for excellence in design in steel. These works were utilized in performances by groups such as the Lotus dance group in Chicago, a project documented in a film now held in the Smithsonian Archives. Her interdisciplinary approach and use of industrial materials align with the era's focus on art-technology collaborations, such as those promoted by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Galen served as Associate Professor Emerita of Theatre Design/Technology at Purchase College (SUNY) from 1981 to 1997 and has taught at New York University, Columbia University, and Ben-Gurion University. She holds a BA from the University of Pennsylvania and an MA from New York University, and she studied at the Art Students League under Morris Kantor. She was married to the abstract painter and publisher Ed Colker.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (born Kenneth Marsh) is a pioneering video artist and producer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known as the co-founder of the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City, established in June 1970 alongside Elliot Glass. The abbreviation 'PVT' for this organization strongly aligns with the 'Pvt' portion of the name provided. Marsh was a key figure in the early video art movement, which shared significant ideological and technical overlaps with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He is documented as having collaborated with E.A.T., and some archival records list him in connection with their publications and projects. Marsh advocated for the use of video as a tool for social feedback and 'video journalism,' capturing historic events such as the first Women's Liberation March and Gay Pride March in New York City. He authored influential technical manuals including 'Independent Video' (1974) and 'The Way the New Technology Works' (1982). In 1972, he moved to Woodstock, New York, where he founded Woodstock Community Video, and in 1973, he established the Artists' TV Lab.
